payload.text:
Crie um website romântico, interativo e responsivo, otimizado para mobile (prioridade) e desktop, com animações suaves, transições imersivas e experiência emocional, inspirado em sites virais vistos no Instagram.
🎵 SEÇÃO 1 — TELA INICIAL (INSPIRAÇÃO SPOTIFY)
Tela fullscreen.
Design inspirado na interface do Spotify.
Mostrar:
Capa de uma música romântica (usar imagem genérica do Google).
Texto: “Nossa música favorita”.
Botão grande: ▶ Tocar.
Ao clicar:
A música começa a tocar no próprio site.
Barra de progresso animada.
Fundo com gradiente suave e leve efeito de partículas.
Texto secundário discreto:
“Essa música sempre nos leva de volta a tudo que vivemos…”
Scroll inicia a próxima experiência.
🎤 SEÇÃO 2 — TRANSIÇÃO “TURNÊ / SHOW”
Conforme o usuário desce a página:
O design muda como se fosse uma turnê musical.
Fundo escuro com luzes suaves, flashes e animações de palco.
Textos grandes surgindo com efeito fade/slide, como:
“Cada momento foi um show inesquecível”
“Cada dia, uma nova canção da nossa história”
A música continua tocando suavemente.
📸 SEÇÃO 3 — MEMÓRIAS (HISTÓRIAS / STORIES)
O design se transforma em uma linha de memórias.
Inspirado no estilo de stories do Instagram e lembranças do Facebook.
Cards em formato vertical (stories).
Usuário pode:
Arrastar para o lado (swipe horizontal).
Cada card contém:
Imagem genérica romântica (Google).
Data fictícia.
Pequena legenda emocional.
Indicadores de progresso no topo, como stories reais.
⏱️ SEÇÃO 4 — CONTADOR DE VIDA (NOSSO FILHO)
Fundo claro, minimalista e emocional.
Título grande: “Desde o dia que nossa vida mudou para sempre…”
Cronômetro animado mostrando:
Tempo desde 10/12/2025 (data de nascimento do nosso filho).
Exibir em:
dias
horas
minutos
segundos
Abaixo, uma frase bonita, por exemplo:
“O amor ganhou um novo nome, um novo sentido e um novo coração.”
📖 SEÇÃO 5 — LIVRO DO AMOR (INTERATIVO)
Design muda completamente para um livro aberto.
Tela dividida em duas páginas:
Página esquerda: imagem.
Página direita: outra imagem ou texto curto.
Efeito realista de:
Folhear páginas
Voltar páginas
Animação suave de papel.
Cada “página” representa um capítulo da história do casal.
Todas as imagens são temporárias (Google), com fácil troca futura.
🎨 ESTILO GERAL
Tipografia elegante e romântica.
Paleta de cores:
tons suaves, rosé, creme, preto elegante e dourado discreto.
Animações leves (scroll-based).
Música nunca reinicia ao trocar de seção.
Performance otimizada.
Código organizado e comentado.
🧠 OBJETIVO
Criar um site presente emocional, perfeito para o Dia 14 (Dia do Amor), que conte uma história através de música, memórias, tempo e amor, como uma experiência imersiva e inesquecível.

**Defining the Objective**

I'm currently focused on defining the project's core objective: a React 18-based, mobile-first web application designed to be romantic, interactive, and highly responsive. The target audience is, as of now, for Valentine's Day (or Day 14), thus my thoughts are centered around the desired user experience.


**Outlining the Structure**

I've been solidifying the application's structure. Thinking through key sections is paramount: a Spotify-style intro, a concert transition, story-like memories, and a minimalist life counter, all while keeping mobile responsiveness and romantic aesthetics in mind. Now, my thoughts are concentrated on integrating TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Lucide React icons, and Framer Motion for the UI's smooth transitions.


**Refining the Life Counter**

I'm now zeroing in on the life counter's implementation, making it time-aware and date-specific, while gracefully handling "time since" or "time until."  The date is 10/12/2025. It'll also have a comment in the code to clarify future-date logic, to make it robust. I'm also ensuring the music maintains persistence with global state. I have the types file set up to define interfaces for memories and the love book. I'm focused on the UI elements to have smooth animations.


**Detailing the Components**

I'm now detailing each component, starting with a music player and its UI, including an album art blur and a prominent play button, all controlled by global audio state. Section 2 uses Framer Motion for text reveals. Section 3 will utilize a horizontal scroll container or Framer Motion for stories. I'm focusing on their respective UI features, interactions, and aesthetics.


**Visualizing the Life Counter**

I'm now visualizing the life counter, considering the optimal UI for manual swipes and visual indicators at the top. It seems best to make it user-controllable, given the swipe prompt. I'm leveraging the provided date of October 12, 2025, for the life counter's logic and adding comments in the code to clarify future date logic, to ensure it works well in the future.

**Refining the Book Section**

I am now thinking about Section 5, the "book" component, and whether to use external libraries to easily implement 3d transforms. I have decided to implement a simplified book interaction using Framer Motion's `AnimatePresence` with custom variants for page turning. This will provide reliability while satisfying the project's code constraints, and will provide a nice experience for the user.


**Planning the Resources**

I'm now determining essential resources: `framer-motion` for animations is confirmed, along with `picsum.photos` with seeds for consistent images. My thoughts now move to setting the correct date for the project, and ensuring any date-related logic works as expected. I'll need to account for date differences, displaying either "Counting down to..." or "Time since..." depending on whether the target date is in the future. I am devising a step-by-step implementation plan.


**Implementing the Elements**

I'm now fully immersed in coding. I've set up the core files: `SectionBook.tsx` for the book component, `App.tsx` for orchestration, and `index.tsx` for mounting. The main focus is the music player's playback and styling ("Rose, Cream, Black, Gold"). I'll simulate a 3D book flip with a stack effect using `rotateY`, for a smooth experience. I will use a copyright-free MP3 link, instead of hardcoding any file paths, and then review against the requirements.

**Adjusting Scrolling Sensitivity**

I'm currently focused on the complaints about the application's scrolling sensitivity. The user is finding the scrolling too rapid and oversensitive. A small input results in a disproportionately large movement, leading to missed sections. I'm thinking about how to refine the responsiveness to better match user expectations.


**Revising Scroll Behavior**

I'm now diving into the "one page per scroll" request. The current CSS `scroll-snap-stop: always` seems promising as a potential solution, which is designed exactly to force stopping at a snap point. I'm going to test its effectiveness against strong momentum scrolling and see if it stops exactly at the next section, even with a strong scroll gesture.


**Implementing Precise Snapping**

I'm now zeroing in on the finer points of scroll-snapping. I'm aiming to prevent skipping snap points even with high scroll momentum. Tailwind's `snap-always` seems like the correct choice for forcing stops. After checking browser support and verifying class names, I'm focusing on modifying the `App.tsx` file and adding `snap-y snap-mandatory` to the scroll container and `snap-always` to each section. I'll make sure each section uses `snap-start`, as well.


**Refining CSS Implementation**

I'm now implementing the `snap-always` property to the child containers within `App.tsx` and focusing on the implications of `snap-mandatory`. I'm also considering `snap-proximity` for broader compatibility, and I have found the correct `scroll-snap-stop: always` to be the best solution. If this approach fails, a JavaScript-based solution may be needed. However, I want to avoid this to maintain performance.


**Verifying CDN Compatibility**

I've confirmed that the CDN version, `https://cdn.tailwindcss.com`, includes `snap-always` from Tailwind CSS v3, so there should be no compatibility issues. Now, I'm going to refine the implementation plan: Specifically, add the `snap-always` class to the wrapper divs in `App.tsx` containing the sections. This directly sets the `scroll-snap-stop: always`, to prevent skipping sections during scroll. I'm verifying that the outer container's `h-screen overflow-y-scroll` is the correct setup.


**Evaluating Section Height**

I'm now assessing the implications of `snap-mandatory` versus the full-screen design. Most sections are set to `h-screen` or `min-h-screen`, suggesting `snap-start` with `snap-always` will provide the desired "fullpage.js" effect. This ensures stopping at the start of each next section, even when content overflows, which matches the user's request to "parar no próximo." I'm confident about these settings. I'm ready to update the files.


**Adding Audio Playback**

I'm now integrating audio playback into the application using a hidden audio element with a persistent loop. I've implemented a `togglePlay` function to handle play/pause functionality and using `useRef` to handle the `audioRef`. Also, I've added a volume control that is shown after the first section is passed. I am ensuring that the audio loops and has a default volume set. The `useEffect` hook ensures the audio settings persist across renders.


**Adding Volume Control**

Now, I'm integrating a volume control button into the application that only appears after the first section is passed. This is being implemented with a conditional rendering approach. The `SectionMusic` component is receiving the `isPlaying`, `togglePlay`, and `audioRef` props, and a button now appears and controls audio using a `useRef` to handle the audio object.


**Finalizing Smooth Scrolling**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the smooth scrolling implementation. After reviewing, `scroll-behavior: smooth` in `index.html` is unnecessary as the `scroll-smooth` class is already on the container in `App.tsx`, and that handles the scrolling physics. The core fix is implementing `snap-always` to prevent skipping sections during scroll. A trackpad with high inertia will ensure smooth stopping with this fix. I'm ready to move to final testing.


**Analyzing Scroll Behavior**

I'm now zeroing in on the behavior when content is larger than the screen. The user's feedback, "eu rolo um pouco avança muito rápido... lá pro final," reinforces the problem of skipping sections. The height of `SectionMusic` is `h-screen` and the rest is `min-h-screen`, which may affect things. The `snap-stop: always` (class `snap-always`) directive, alongside `snap-start`, should make scrolling between sections "sticky" and eliminate this problem by snapping to the top of the next section, even if the user scrolls with considerable momentum. I'm focusing on ensuring each section properly uses `snap-start` for alignment with `snap-always`.


**Refining Implementation Strategy**

I'm now clarifying the application of `snap-always`. The plan is to add the `snap-always` class directly to each section within `App.tsx`. My focus is ensuring these sections stop precisely at the top, preventing the rapid advancement that's been reported. I'm double-checking that each section uses `snap-start` for perfect alignment. My priority is ensuring a smooth, predictable scroll experience, especially with increased momentum.


**Implementing Audio Logic**

I'm now integrating the audio logic into the existing sections. I've focused on ensuring the audio correctly loops and has persistent volume settings. The play/pause function and the `useEffect` hook are both working as expected, and now I've integrated a volume control visible after the first section.


**Verifying Style Conflicts**

I'm now methodically checking for conflicting styles within `index.html`. Specifically, I'm examining the `body` and `html` elements for potential overrides to the intended scroll behavior, particularly any that involve `overflow-x: hidden`. I'll be certain no global styles are interfering with my planned implementation.


**Refining Scroll Container**

I'm now revising the scroll container setup for optimal behavior, focusing on the `h-screen` and `overflow-y-scroll` configurations. I'm adding `overscroll-y-contain` to the container. It should prevent visual bouncing on certain devices, even though `snap-mandatory` should handle the resting points of each section. The `snap-y` and `snap-mandatory` classes, plus `snap-always` on the children, should create the desired effect.


**Confirming Section Snapping**

I'm checking the `snap-always` and `snap-start` implementation. Adding `overscroll-y-contain` to the container addresses potential visual artifacts, and I am confident that these configurations will prevent skipping sections, even with fast scrolling, thus addressing the user's issue with momentum. I'm focusing now on the correct placement of `snap-start` and the behavior of the `h-screen` sections.


**Refining Audio Integration**

I'm now integrating the audio logic, including volume control, within each individual section to allow for a better interface. I am using a conditional rendering approach based on whether or not the section is in view. I'm testing the volume control and play/pause functionality, which are both essential components in the design.


**Optimizing Volume Control**

I'm now integrating volume control directly within each section of the app for a more intuitive interface. I'm focusing on conditional rendering based on section visibility and ensuring the audio controls, particularly play/pause and volume, function seamlessly within each individual section. I am making progress and now testing the full integration of these controls.

Para resolver o problema da rolagem muito rápida que pula seções, precisamos ajustar o comportamento do "Scroll Snap" (alinhamento de rolagem).

**O que será feito:**
1.  **Adicionar `snap-always` (CSS `scroll-snap-stop: always`):** Esta propriedade é a chave. Ela força o navegador a parar **obrigatoriamente** no próximo elemento `snap-start`, impedindo que um movimento forte de rolagem pule várias seções de uma vez (como ir do início direto para o final).
2.  **Adicionar `overscroll-y-contain`:** Ajuda a conter a rolagem dentro do container, evitando efeitos elásticos indesejados que podem dar a sensação de falta de controle.

**Refining the Animation Goal**

I'm now zeroing in on the core goal: enhancing the page-turning animation in `SectionBook.tsx`. The aim is definitely to achieve a more realistic paper effect and, crucially, to integrate a subtle sound cue. I need to prioritize these elements to create a more engaging user experience, which leads me to focus on animation techniques and sound integration.


**Approximating the Turn**

I'm now focusing on approximating a 3D page turn using existing tools. My strategy centers on using `rotateY` transforms within a container with a `perspective` to create the illusion of a flipped page. I'm targeting a mobile-first, responsive design within the existing Tailwind, React, and Framer Motion setup. The plan is for the current page to rotate and fade while the new page simultaneously rotates into view.


**Implementing the Back Turn**

I'm now implementing the reverse animation, inverting the action. A slight shadow during the turn and adjusting the anchor point are things I'm investigating to give a "paper" feel, along with a clean 3D rotation with a centered "hinge". I will work on integrating the proper sound effect triggered by the flip actions.


**Refining the Animation Logic**

I'm now focusing on how the rotation should happen, specifically from the spine of the book. For desktop, this means a central hinge, with left pages rotating on the right edge and right pages on the left. The `SectionBook` renders a spread as a single block, making a rigid board turn incorrect. I am now exploring an effect that makes the entering view slide/rotate on top of the old one, and it will be a "Flip" effect. The next step is old page rotating -90/90 and the new page entering from 90/-90. I will integrate a constant, `PAGE_FLIP_SOUND`. I need to add `const [direction, setDirection] =` to the code.


**Coding the Updates**

I'm now in the thick of code implementation! I've added the sound URL to `constants.ts`. The `SectionBook.tsx` component now imports the sound and includes audio refs for instantiation. The `nextPage` and `prevPage` functions are updated to control the sound and the flip direction. I've updated `AnimatePresence` with `custom={direction}` and the `motion.div` variants to properly handle the 3D rotation based on the flip direction, and added `transform-style: preserve-3d` to the container.

**Perfecting the Paper Feel**

I've been refining the animation physics. `easeInOut` with a duration of 0.6s is a good starting point for the paper feel. Also, the anchor point is now centered to the spine. The transition is now focusing on the requested `rotateY` change.
